mnemonics: Structure: S for Same data type grouping. Union: U for Using one member at a time.
Struct is like a backpack with separate pockets for all your items. Union is like a multi-tool where you can only use one tool at a time.
S.U.R.E. - Structures Unite Related Elements. Unions Reserve Exclusive memory.
keyPoints: A structure (struct) allocates storage for all its members simultaneously, allowing each to be accessed independently.
A union allocates storage for its largest member only, and all members share the same memory location, meaning only one member can hold a value at any given time.
Unions are memory-efficient for storing different data types that are not used concurrently, while structures are used for grouping related data of different types that are all needed together.
commonMistakes: Treating a union as a structure and attempting to use all members simultaneously, which leads to corrupted data.
Forgetting that the size of a structure is the sum of its members (plus padding), while the size of a union is the size of its largest member.
Using a union member without initializing it first, resulting in undefined behavior because the memory might contain garbage values from a previously stored type.
